title: Reducing water use when washing a car at home
subtitle: Buckets, waterless products, and a hosepipe permit: where the water actually goes
excerpt: A hosepipe left running to wash a car uses 300 to 500 litres. A two-bucket wash uses 20 to 30 litres. A waterless wash product uses under 1 litre. The difference matters both in metered households and in hosepipe ban areas. This covers each method, water volumes, and where hosepipe restrictions apply.
sourceNotes: Waterwise UK car washing water usage data. Environment Agency water use in the home statistics. Which? car washing water comparison.

glossaryTerms.definition: A temporary water restriction imposed by a water company under the Water Industry Act 1991, prohibiting use of a hosepipe for watering gardens, filling paddling pools, or washing vehicles. Fines of up to £1,000 apply.

content.text: Car washing is responsible for around 5% of outdoor domestic water use in England during summer. In a metered household, a hosepipe wash is not just an environmental issue: at typical metered water rates, a 400-litre hosepipe wash costs 60 to 80p per wash. Twice per month that adds up. Switching to a two-bucket method uses around 5% as much water for a minimal additional cost.
Hosepipe running continuously: 300 to 500 litres. A standard garden hosepipe flows at 15 to 20 litres per minute; a 20-minute wash uses 300 to 400 litres. Subject to hosepipe ban prohibition.

Two-bucket method: 20 to 30 litres. One bucket with soapy water for washing, one bucket of clean water for rinsing the mitt. Produces a comparable clean result if a quality car shampoo is used. Buckets are not covered by hosepipe ban restrictions.

Waterless car wash product: under 1 litre. A spray-on waterless product (Bilt Hamber Waterless Wash, Chemical Guys Waterless Wash, or similar) is wiped over the car surface and buffed off. Effective for light dust and bird dropping removal between full washes. Not suitable as a sole cleaning method when the car has heavy road grime or mud.

Rainwater from a butt: 0 additional mains water. A 200-litre water butt collects enough for 6 to 8 bucket washes from one heavy rainfall. Rainwater is soft and leaves fewer spots than hard tap water in chalk and limestone areas.

prohibits using a hosepipe to wash a private vehicle. Buckets, watering cans, and hand-held containers are not covered. A trigger-stop nozzle on a hosepipe does not exempt it from the ban: the prohibition is on connecting a hosepipe to the tap and using it for vehicle washing, regardless of how the water is discharged. Commercial car washes that use recirculated water are typically exempt. The restrictions and any exemptions are published on each water company's website when a ban is in force.
